sections.title: Contributor Instructions
sections.content:
If you have been given access to this document as a contributor, welcome aboard! 

Simply add your content directly to this document. Everything is saved automatically.

Please edit this document directly. Copying/Pasting from Word etc is likely to break things.

  • Read the Google Docs Cheat Sheet

  • Edit the content inside the email section,
  • below Subject 
  • and above the “Best of the Internet” section

  • Multiple people can edit at the same time

  • Headings
  • When editing - make sure to use proper headings for each section
  • Add headings as required - make sure to use the Heading 1 style for each heading (click the drop down that says “Normal text”)
  • Sub headings within your article should be used from Heading 2 downwards as required.
  • Please try to add your “Author Name re kiports” heading (h5) under your main article heading

  • Images
  • Please do include images in your article - instructions
  • Try to set the width of your image so its no wider than the page

  • Testing
  • You can preview the email layout by pressing the blue M in a circle next to the “to” section, this will create the email.
  • You can also choose to send it to your personal address if you want to test it there.

sections.title: Newsletter Instructions
sections.content:
These instructions are only for the person actually creating/sending the newsletter.

  • Create a copy of the template file and rename it as YYYY-DD, for example 2022–08
  • Right click the template file, select “Make a copy”
  • Right click the newly created copy and select “rename” and set to the edition date in YYYY-DD format

  • Editing - see above

  • Final Preparations
  • Check images are all no wider than the document
  • Apply headings where authors have not
  • Check all articles have the “author name reports” sub heading
  • Insert “front page” image
  • Prompt members to use the form https://accounts.google.com/v3/signin/identifier?continue=https%3A%2F%2Fdocs.google.com%2Fforms%2Fd%2Fe%2F1FAIpQLSe79ojLfz6TsCSMi1Ehm_sg4aDlw4d5RL--yYwzmrV0QsVMIg%2Fviewform&dsh=S-83452649%3A1783500342192048&followup=https%3A%2F%2Fdocs.google.com%2Fforms%2Fd%2Fe%2F1FAIpQLSe79ojLfz6TsCSMi1Ehm_sg4aDlw4d5RL--yYwzmrV0QsVMIg%2Fviewform<mpl=forms&osid=1&passive=1209600&service=wise&flowName=WebLiteSignIn&flowEntry=ServiceLogin&ifkv=AcDsRvwcQbU12lQ1osZAulEQE4rj0mscgYnCcScEfDSXGsTtt7tO63zDA9G6mKR_nsV_Qfa4phg2
  • Choose an image from the form for the front
  • Insert->Image->Drive and choose the photo
  • Add “other photos submitted” final heading for anything submitted but didn’t make the cover
  • Insert->Image->Drive and select all newly submitted photos but the cover one

  • Sending
  • Press the blue gmail icon to generate an email
  • WARNING chrome might block the popup. Check for an icon on the right hand side of the address bar. You need to ensure popups are not blocked for Google Drive
  • Fill in recipients etc within the email and press send as normal
  • Test send to a private email address first
  • Once happy, send to the membermojo group email address - can only be done from an approved address
  • Address to send to is members.dhpc@membermojo.co.uk
  • Can only be sent by a pre approved email address in membermojo

  • Creating PDF
  • Use a non personal gmail account (eg I have a josephparagliding@gmail.com account for pg related stuff)
  • Clear out the To section of the email form 
  • Open the email preview and select “print”, but choose “print as PDF”
  • Save the PDF file name YYYY-DD-Skywords.pdf
  • Upload the file to this folder (Skywords / EDITIONS / Skywords Archive Public Share PDFs)

To

members.dhpc@membermojo.co.uk

Cc

Bcc

Subject

Skywords - February 2023

Dear [first_name],

Skywords - February 2024

The newsletter of the Dales Hang gliding and Paragliding Club

Social - Informal Club Night Tonight,

Horse and Farrier Otley 7:30pm

Stef Sykes reports

Club Social is on tonight but with only one film and two photos submitted we're going to have to abandon the Film and Photo Competition this year. That means no formal presentation, but people are still welcome to come along and socialise.

Thanks to those who submitted and I'm sorry the rest of us didn't pull our weight. I know many are out of the country or busy with work this time of year... Anyway, the bar will be open but Mikes Library of Dreams not.

Coming just two weeks after Mr Meeks presentation has perhaps worn us out.

Next month, please put in your diary the 7th of March when the famous John 'Westie' Westall shall train us in his winning Hike n Fly techniques.

All the best and hope to see you on the hill.

Chairman's Chat

Martin Baxter reports

The first meeting of your new committee, planned for 17 Jan, didn’t go particularly well.  In fact, I took the decision to cancel the meeting when it became apparent that only 4 out of 15 committee members would be able to attend.  Excuses were fairly pathetic, and varied from being on the wrong side of the world (Colombia) to being hospitalised.  Get well soon Tam.

We hadn’t met for 4 months so there had been quite a lot to talk about.  Rather than postponing the meeting we decided to move business to the committee only (secret squirrel) area of the forum.  This is normally fairly effective but takes a little longer, so I’m not able to report any earth-shattering decisions just yet.  That means that I’m going to inflict another chapter of my military flying career upon you in this edition.  I won’t be at all offended if you’ve got something better to do…

Previous chapters, for insomniacs, as follows:

Chapter 1 - Sycamore Seeds

Chapter 2 - Parachuting

Chapter 3 - Reserve Deployment

Chapter 4 - Alcohol Makes Friends

Happy Hookers

As I mentioned in chapter 1, I’d circulated with military pilots throughout the early part of my career.  A colleague had told me that he had applied to become an Army pilot: get paid whilst somebody teaches you to fly.  I duly submitted my application form, fully expecting to get turned down.  My desk officer (HR in civilian speak) told me that there was plenty of time for that (I’ll explain why he was wrong in a later article), and that I could squeeze in a ‘fun job’ first.  At the time I was too naïve to realise that his priority was to fill job vacancies rather than managing my career.  So, in my innocence, as a young Captain, I took command of the Joint Helicopter Support Unit (JHSU) at RAF Odiham.  My handover/takeover took a foreboding turn when my predecessor told me that he often found himself sitting in his room with a shotgun in his mouth!  Some months later I heard that he had committed suicide.  Fortunately (for me) his depression was all about family life and not the job.

JHSU was one of the first units to mix servicemen (still no women in those days) from different services.  About two thirds of my unit were Army and one third were RAF.  Our job was to support the RAF Support Helicopter Force which consisted of Pumas and Chinooks.  This mostly involved rigging Underslung Loads using chains or nets: everything from military vehicles to sand bags for shoring up river banks during flooding.  We were happy to be referred to as ‘hookers’ when attaching these loads to the aircraft.  We also set up field landing sites for use by day and by night.  One specialist piece of equipment was the Bardic Angle of Approach Indicator (AAI).  Set up correctly it would enable aircraft to fly down a beam of light avoiding obstacles: a portable (and very cheap) version of Instrument Landing System used by modern commercial aircraft.  If the pilot saw an amber light it meant he was too high (hot), a red light indicated that he was too low, and the Goldilocks light was green.

One team was permanently deployed to the Falkland Islands on a rotational basis with our sister unit in Germany.  We also had a very small training wing where we trained Helicopter Handling Instructors and teams.

The downwash from a Chinook helicopter carrying a heavy load is not to be sniffed at.  I remember watching one course member, who lost his balance, being blown away like a cartwheel.  Pilots were often getting into trouble for destroying tentage and blowing over portaloos – sometimes with unlucky occupants inside!

Our hangar was next to the helicopter refuelling point.  This made it almost impossible to hold a conversation whilst the aircraft conducted rotors running refuels, but it did make an excellent spot for picking mushrooms: the downwash did a great job of spreading the spores.

Whilst we didn’t usually have the luxury of travelling by air, we did get benefits in kind.  On one occasion we practiced abseiling out of a Puma from about 200’.  Ear defenders were essential because when you climb out of the door your head is right next to one of the noisy gas turbine engines.  For obvious reasons it’s also important that the pilot can maintain a constant height.  I honestly can’t remember if we tied a knot at the end of each rope!

Like bumble bees, helicopters defy the laws of nature, and have no right to take to the skies.  It really isn’t advisable during flight to ponder on the outcome of multiplying the number of critical components by their average reliability.  Sitting in the back of a Chinook when it’s flying isn’t too bad, if a tad noisy; but it’s a different experience between rotor start and flight idle.  As the rotors speed up resonance takes a temporary hold in each of the 3 dimensions.  It’s a wonder that the vibrations don’t shake the aircraft to pieces.

As ‘occasional’ crew we were introduced to the Underwater Escape Training Unit, affectionately known as the ‘Dunker’.  The idea is to practise escaping from an aircraft that has ditched in the sea.  Following a briefing you all get strapped into a mock-up of a helicopter and then the whole thing gets dunked into a swimming pool.  Once the movement stops and the bubbles clear you unclip your harness and, in turn, swim out of the nearest exit.  Everybody experiences 3 runs: once upright, once upside down, and once upside down in pitch darkness.  One of the tricks is to position you hand on something solid that is in the direction of your nearest exit, and then follow that hand.  Otherwise, it’s all too easy to get disorientated and swim in the wrong direction.  I often relate this to frequently checking the position of your reserve handle whilst paragliding.

Helicopters generate static electricity.  Every aircraft had to be discharged by touching it with a grounded metal rod before attaching or detaching the load.  Not as easy as it sounds in the dark under a large helicopter with a one-ton load swinging around underneath it.  The American CH-53, with its 7 bladed main rotor, was apparently particularly bad in this aspect.  We were told about servicemen who had lost fingers as a result of the discharge.

 

Photo: April 1991 - Delivering humanitarian aid to Kurdish refugees in northern Iraq.  View from the inside of a Chinook CH47.

When Saddam Hussein invaded Kuwait in 1990 half of my unit deployed as part of the coalition, tasked with slapping his wrist.  You may recall that this didn’t go quite according to plan and Saddam decided to take it out on the Kurds in northern Iraq.  They had little option but to flee into the mountains.  I deployed, at short notice, with the rest of my unit and a number of Chinook helicopters, to a base on the Turkey/Syria/Iraq border where we delivered humanitarian aid to the Kurdish refugees.  Little did I know that I’d be returning to Kuwait and Iraq just over a decade later for round 2, but that’s another story.

JHSU was one of the happiest and highly motivated units that I have served in.  Whilst I like to think that this was down to my leadership, the truth is that we were respected specialists who were mostly left to our own devices.  My Army boss was many miles away and my RAF boss lived on the other side of the airfield.  He came in very handy when it came to resubmitting my application for the Army Pilots’ Course.  As a helicopter instructor himself it was hard to ignore his letter of support.

 

Next time – Becoming an Army Pilot.

Sites and News

Martin Baxter reports

Next time you are parawaiting at Wether Fell, impress your fellow pilots by informing them that a ‘wether’ is a male sheep that has been castrated before reaching maturity.  Parabollox indeed.

Safety

Clive Sury reports

Reserve Repack Day - Sunday 14th January

Well that’s another Dales Club Repack Day done.  A good turnout with 23 flyers attending.  We were in the Gymnasium this time and not the B Hall at Ilkley Grammar School.  Luckily everyone found the right entrance - if some eventually!

Guy Richardsons, Ginger Nomad, led the team of 4 guides to help us and were busy all day.  It proved a good move to have split sessions in the morning and afternoon as this allowed enough room for people to repack in pairs without waiting around for space.  Plus, ensured that the the support team could give attention to more people.  Where possible people were paired up with others that had the same or similar reserve makes and models, and this too aided operations.

It was great to see the teamwork and help people gave each other, as well as, the sociability.  Plus, I’d like to say thank you to everyone for their patience and help when we had to reset the room from the initial confusion with the children's gym class thinking that they’d booked the place.  Things like this can happen with an innocent misunderstanding by the children’s gymnastic coach but this was all sorted out very amicably and we started 20 mins late waiting for some of our own group and not down to this.  Also, thanks everyone for ensuring the gym was clean and tidy throughout and at the end of the day - really helps.

It’s only the 4th time I’ve repacked my reserve and each time is a massive learning curve.  I’m getting better at it and this year had a new reserve to repack.  It’s a Gin G-Lite and proved quite difficult to repack back down from being opened up.  It’s highly non-porous and the material is very thin and slippery.  Great for what it’s designed for, it just took 3 pairs of hands to get into my Supair reserve bag!  Days like this are invaluable in understanding your equipment and learning from others.  One point Ginge made was that when choosing a reserve, people most often ask about price, weight, square or round but hardly ever how easy it is to pack!  One point to think about and I know one flyer on the day appreciated finding out that theirs is one of the easiest.

A few learning points to share with others are the following:

  • Ensure you have repack instructions for your reserve accessible - preferably printed.  What would you do on a trip if you didn’t have this, and essential for the repack day.
  • If you don’t have one, consider buying a spare reserve bag.  Again, what would you do if you used your reserve and didn’t have a bag to put in it to reuse on a trip?
  • Check your reserve connectors.  On the day we saw mailons that were not screwed tightly enough, and others where the connection could move on the maillon, risking added tension which could cause breakage.
  • Reserves can degrade before their recommended expiry term of use.  Therefore, physically inspecting them is essential.  
  • Know your weight for the reserve to be used.  Too light could lead to instability, too heavy could lead to a more severe descent rate or even, the reserve imploding.
  • Many manufacturers recommend repacking every 6 months.  This is not just for inspection but also so that the reserve is opened out and aired, thereby not as ‘sticky’ and opens at its optimum rate.  Each microsecond of time to open in deployment could be vital.  Professional repacks involve the reserve being aired for 24 hours.
  • Guy (Ginger Nomad) and James (Airofix) can do professional repack with certificates which may be required for some organised trips and SIV’s.  These days are a good opportunity to drop off or collect wings and kit for them, even if you’re not repacking your reserve on the day itself.
  • Finally, Guy and James organise donations of condemned reserves to a charity in Africa that use them for mercy flight drops. Apparently, donators are even notified of where and when their reserve is used this one last time.  You may wish to send any unusable reserves to them for this worthwhile cause.

Again, thanks to Guy, James, Christina and Bernard for the support and invaluable guidance.  Plus, to everyone on the day making it a very positive knowledge-building experience.  See you next year.

P.S.  There were 2 left items from the day.  Below photos.  If yours, please contact me direct via Whatsapp or Telegram.

How to manage an incident on the hill

This is an article from Cross Country Magazine.  It’s highly informative and important for everyone to know how they can help and what their role can be to support others in a critical situation.  Click on the picture to be directed to the online article.

Safe flying,

Clive.

A New Section in the Pilot Exam

Paul Rowntree reports

Last month I re-sat two sections of the new online pilot exam.  One of them is a new section called Human Factors and Airmanship.  The reason for the addition is the causes of accidents in today’s statistics.  The number of accidents caused by faulty equipment is now very low and increased training and knowledge of the sport has decreased accidents caused by lack of understanding, but the accident rate is the same.  The reason for this is the rise in accidents caused by our decision making.

This link is a 9-page document recently issued by the BHPA highlighting a number of points we probably/hopefully give subconscious thought to before we go flying, but I am sure we are all guilty of pushing the limits of some of them at times.  

From the Archive

Martin Baxter reports

New Old Skywords

Tam reports

Sometime last year a batch of old hard copy black and white editions of Skywords were found (in the library of all places - who’d have thunk it). These date from 2001 - 2004, a time when HG was more prevalent, Trev was Chairman, we had foot and mouth interfering with flying, new fangled things being introduced by Pete Logan - like email groups, and some online information.  There was useful instruction, like how to calculate your XC distance using OS Grid references, and lots of interesting facts - paraglider pilots were once known as jellyfish by HG pilots for instance. You can even find out Fred Winstanley’s favourite pastime. So they are worth a look, and have been added to the online library: https://www.dhpc.org.uk/library/skywords-archive.  Some things haven’t changed much of course. Jake Herbert leading the PG XC League, closely followed by Chris Fountain, for instance. You’ll have to ask Fred yourself if you want to know if his favourite pastime has changed.

                                         Skytraxx 5 Review

Ed Cleasby reviews

This my personal opinion of the latest Skytraxx 5, with the popular Skytraxx 2.1 model  used for comparison.

First a bit of backstory as I feel it’s important to understand where dedicated flight instruments now sit, given the dominance of free flight apps, and whether full-fat, dedicated flight instruments are still relevant.

What went before?

For years I flew with the excellent Flymaster … whichever  model you had, they were quality instruments that did everything, but tended to be slow and clunky to use, especially for tasking. Then, about a decade ago, smartphones  began to appear on flightdecks. From around 2012/13 Flyskyhy (iOS) and xctrack (Android) apps came onto the scene and this revolutionised navigation and airspace display along with superb accompanying mapping. Pre-dating them as a sort of intermediate step were xcsoar/LK8000.  In short, despite issues centred on battery life and sunlight visibility, the new apps were way ahead in terms of ease of use, sharing and customisability - but no usable vario. But above all it was the high quality mapping they introduced.  No dedicated instrument, even now, can come close, although a few like the Air 3 piggy-back xctrack,  but at the expense of bulk and weight. Given where things were going, any manufacturer of dedicated flight instruments had an up-hill battle to compete.  Naviter and Flymaster tried; still trying, but with limited success and now seem to be switching to a similar approach to the xctracer … a smaller, compact vario paired with a phone and integrating FLARM, or trying to raise the level of the maps.

A couple of years back, encouraged by the excellent CAA rebate (no-one should have looked this government gift-horse in the mouth) I sold my Flymaster and looked at the options.  Really it boiled down to two … the xctracer maxx and the Skytraxx 2.1. Both had excellent reviews, but for me the Skytraxx offered so much more at the same price; in short, full navigation, airspace and what has always been important to me; it was a fully-fledged backup to my phone (I never feel comfortable with all my eggs in one basket).  My tendency is to always plug in a route (I can’t say I execute many, but I need that focus) and the Skytraxx provides easy tasking, with an igc file having both a g and  c stamp (for declared flights)..  When I bought the 2.1, I pushed for the c record to be added for the UKXL, within two weeks Skytraxx responded with an update. Brilliant! It’s now standard.

My experience of the 2.1 over a number of seasons has been excellent – hard to fault. It’s been totally reliable, easy to use and It's small, light and runs forever - 30hrs/charge. The vario is very responsive, it’s simple to customise, add screens to  - and I like orange. Name another instrument that comes with a bright orange rubber protective cover. It also had (note the use of had) FANET+ hence EC to qualify for the rebate.

Why switch to the Skytraxx 5?

The initial jolt was on discovering that Skytraxx, like other manufacturers, had problems sourcing components to the extent that all models in the Skytraxx  range, from the 2.1 up to and including the 3 and full colour, 4 variants were being discontinued. You can only find second-hand now and they command a premium. A bit of digging also revealed that whilst they were still providing  support, it didn’t cover some parts like screens, keypads or odd chips they couldn’t get hold of.  Hence, the Skytraxx 5 is actually a very different beast in terms of its  internal architecture, with a different keyboard and screen. Basically, a full redesign. However, it keeps all the familiar user features, menu structure and layout of the 2.1, but with some extras including full FLARM, sharing via BT, live tracking and  the Skysafe filter which looks especially useful. Upgrading to full FLARM  means other FLARM users (not just FANET+) appear on the map screen. To avoid clutter, such as with extraneous waypoints, you can be selective in terms of who/what you choose to make visible.

 

What do I think of it?

The first noticeable thing is the screen – another big reason I bought it.  When you put the 2.1 and the 5 side by side (see top photo) the 5 is  larger, sharper and brighter. It’s even more obvious in the flesh.  It’s been slimmed down to a mere 15mm but weight-wise it’s added about 50g to 200gm. Various screen layouts come as defaults, including the Classic 1 from the 2.1, but a new Classic 2 becomes the main start-up screen.You can design as many screen profiles as you need and store them for selection. Within each profile is a nest of screens, all customisable and as many as you wish. One change I’m not a fan of, although you rarely need to use it except to set up your pilot/wing profile, is the navigation around the alphanumeric keyboard. Star Wars fans might love it. It's a gyroscopic tilting wonder to move a small dot … I think I’m pretty good at steady hand games, but I’ve still to develop the knack of moving that darned ball with precision!  The outer case is a sort of hard, non removal rubber - no orange flip off.

Another selling point for me was the vario. The 2.1 is very good, both in terms of responsiveness and having a nice, rising tone. Over the years I played around with the ‘tone editor’ and came up with several ‘Ed’ tones, but in the end I couldn’t better the default.. I did a number of side by side tests and right away it was obvious the 5 is even more responsive and the sound much cleaner. At 50% it’s more than loud enough … a few had complained the 2.1 was rather quiet, although I never found that to be the case running it at 80%.volume I would say the vario is the equal of the xctracer. …. which means the best.

A small addition, not found on the 2.1, is the ‘double tap’ that can be set to go back or forwards through the screens. Whether I’d use it is another matter.

The same four keyboard buttons are retained, although slightly larger and raised slightly – presumably for gloved hands. I actually found the 2.1 keyboard quicker,  the 5 requires a definite push and the scroll speed is slower.  

A few small changes - the hike mode is now subsumed into the main flight logging directory, previously it was separate  and a pain if you were into hike and fly.  I spent XDales time stitching them together!  Now it seems to know if you’re hiking, resting or flying - apparently. A small, but a  useful thing to  note is it now accepts both .wpt and gpx formats. The 2.1 was solely gpx.

These are my initial impressions. I’ve yet to get any flying weather to try it out, but overall I’m really glad I took the opportunity to upgrade. As far as I know there are no dealers in the UK … post Brexit wrecks margins. You’ll need to go direct which can bring its own wallet pain (14 euro to ship in the EU, 50 euro to the UK + duty). Set against  that the rebate is still available until 31st March, but the window is closing.fast.   Like a number of manufacturers there is also a subscription element … Burnair is 39 euro/year although that can be reduced to 20 euro if you opt for a longer period. There is a good online manual (a written one is included, although far less detailed).which explains the benefits, but I don’t see it’s worth it to have live weather, instant flight uploads to X Contest, live tracking etc.  It comes with a sim card inside and the first three months are free so you can try before you decide. However, I will possibly upgrade to full FLARM for a one-off 79 euro.

In my opinion the Skytraxx 5 is well worth considering and you’re very welcome to check out mine. At the end of the day you pay your money. This in my view, is one of the best value, fully dedicated flight instruments available  at  present.Whether you want to pair with your phone, just use it as a  backup, or as a standalone - it will  do everything  that pesky app will do -  minus the mapping, but with five times the battery life and at the lowest weight of  any comparable instrument.

Club Communications

Tam reports

The club recently adopted the Telegram Chat Group as a formal club group. Previously it had been left out of formal club comms channels, but as time has progressed it has become clear that it is now a key channel, and will be flagged up in future in new members’ welcome packs etc. The committee continues to believe that longer term info should be put on the relevant area of the forum, so at least to avoid having to answer the same questions multiple times.  Best use would suggest putting info on the forum/website and linking to it from the chat group.

The “Club Communication” article on the website has been updated to reflect this change.

Happy flying all.

CONTENT ENDS AT THE LINE ABOVE HERE

INSERT YOUR CONTENT ABOVE THE “Best of the Internet” HEADING

AS THAT IS INTENDED TO BE THE FINAL SECTION

sections.title: Horse and Farrier Otley 7:30pm
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

Club Social is on tonight but with only one film and two photos submitted we're going to have to abandon the Film and Photo Competition this year. That means no formal presentation, but people are still welcome to come along and socialise.

Thanks to those who submitted and I'm sorry the rest of us didn't pull our weight. I know many are out of the country or busy with work this time of year... Anyway, the bar will be open but Mikes Library of Dreams not.

Coming just two weeks after Mr Meeks presentation has perhaps worn us out.

Next month, please put in your diary the 7th of March when the famous John 'Westie' Westall shall train us in his winning Hike n Fly techniques.

All the best and hope to see you on the hill.

sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

The first meeting of your new committee, planned for 17 Jan, didn’t go particularly well.  In fact, I took the decision to cancel the meeting when it became apparent that only 4 out of 15 committee members would be able to attend.  Excuses were fairly pathetic, and varied from being on the wrong side of the world (Colombia) to being hospitalised.  Get well soon Tam.

We hadn’t met for 4 months so there had been quite a lot to talk about.  Rather than postponing the meeting we decided to move business to the committee only (secret squirrel) area of the forum.  This is normally fairly effective but takes a little longer, so I’m not able to report any earth-shattering decisions just yet.  That means that I’m going to inflict another chapter of my military flying career upon you in this edition.  I won’t be at all offended if you’ve got something better to do…

Previous chapters, for insomniacs, as follows:

Chapter 1 - Sycamore Seeds

Chapter 2 - Parachuting

Chapter 3 - Reserve Deployment

Chapter 4 - Alcohol Makes Friends

Happy Hookers

As I mentioned in chapter 1, I’d circulated with military pilots throughout the early part of my career.  A colleague had told me that he had applied to become an Army pilot: get paid whilst somebody teaches you to fly.  I duly submitted my application form, fully expecting to get turned down.  My desk officer (HR in civilian speak) told me that there was plenty of time for that (I’ll explain why he was wrong in a later article), and that I could squeeze in a ‘fun job’ first.  At the time I was too naïve to realise that his priority was to fill job vacancies rather than managing my career.  So, in my innocence, as a young Captain, I took command of the Joint Helicopter Support Unit (JHSU) at RAF Odiham.  My handover/takeover took a foreboding turn when my predecessor told me that he often found himself sitting in his room with a shotgun in his mouth!  Some months later I heard that he had committed suicide.  Fortunately (for me) his depression was all about family life and not the job.

JHSU was one of the first units to mix servicemen (still no women in those days) from different services.  About two thirds of my unit were Army and one third were RAF.  Our job was to support the RAF Support Helicopter Force which consisted of Pumas and Chinooks.  This mostly involved rigging Underslung Loads using chains or nets: everything from military vehicles to sand bags for shoring up river banks during flooding.  We were happy to be referred to as ‘hookers’ when attaching these loads to the aircraft.  We also set up field landing sites for use by day and by night.  One specialist piece of equipment was the Bardic Angle of Approach Indicator (AAI).  Set up correctly it would enable aircraft to fly down a beam of light avoiding obstacles: a portable (and very cheap) version of Instrument Landing System used by modern commercial aircraft.  If the pilot saw an amber light it meant he was too high (hot), a red light indicated that he was too low, and the Goldilocks light was green.

One team was permanently deployed to the Falkland Islands on a rotational basis with our sister unit in Germany.  We also had a very small training wing where we trained Helicopter Handling Instructors and teams.

The downwash from a Chinook helicopter carrying a heavy load is not to be sniffed at.  I remember watching one course member, who lost his balance, being blown away like a cartwheel.  Pilots were often getting into trouble for destroying tentage and blowing over portaloos – sometimes with unlucky occupants inside!

Our hangar was next to the helicopter refuelling point.  This made it almost impossible to hold a conversation whilst the aircraft conducted rotors running refuels, but it did make an excellent spot for picking mushrooms: the downwash did a great job of spreading the spores.

Whilst we didn’t usually have the luxury of travelling by air, we did get benefits in kind.  On one occasion we practiced abseiling out of a Puma from about 200’.  Ear defenders were essential because when you climb out of the door your head is right next to one of the noisy gas turbine engines.  For obvious reasons it’s also important that the pilot can maintain a constant height.  I honestly can’t remember if we tied a knot at the end of each rope!

Like bumble bees, helicopters defy the laws of nature, and have no right to take to the skies.  It really isn’t advisable during flight to ponder on the outcome of multiplying the number of critical components by their average reliability.  Sitting in the back of a Chinook when it’s flying isn’t too bad, if a tad noisy; but it’s a different experience between rotor start and flight idle.  As the rotors speed up resonance takes a temporary hold in each of the 3 dimensions.  It’s a wonder that the vibrations don’t shake the aircraft to pieces.

As ‘occasional’ crew we were introduced to the Underwater Escape Training Unit, affectionately known as the ‘Dunker’.  The idea is to practise escaping from an aircraft that has ditched in the sea.  Following a briefing you all get strapped into a mock-up of a helicopter and then the whole thing gets dunked into a swimming pool.  Once the movement stops and the bubbles clear you unclip your harness and, in turn, swim out of the nearest exit.  Everybody experiences 3 runs: once upright, once upside down, and once upside down in pitch darkness.  One of the tricks is to position you hand on something solid that is in the direction of your nearest exit, and then follow that hand.  Otherwise, it’s all too easy to get disorientated and swim in the wrong direction.  I often relate this to frequently checking the position of your reserve handle whilst paragliding.

Helicopters generate static electricity.  Every aircraft had to be discharged by touching it with a grounded metal rod before attaching or detaching the load.  Not as easy as it sounds in the dark under a large helicopter with a one-ton load swinging around underneath it.  The American CH-53, with its 7 bladed main rotor, was apparently particularly bad in this aspect.  We were told about servicemen who had lost fingers as a result of the discharge.

 

Photo: April 1991 - Delivering humanitarian aid to Kurdish refugees in northern Iraq.  View from the inside of a Chinook CH47.

When Saddam Hussein invaded Kuwait in 1990 half of my unit deployed as part of the coalition, tasked with slapping his wrist.  You may recall that this didn’t go quite according to plan and Saddam decided to take it out on the Kurds in northern Iraq.  They had little option but to flee into the mountains.  I deployed, at short notice, with the rest of my unit and a number of Chinook helicopters, to a base on the Turkey/Syria/Iraq border where we delivered humanitarian aid to the Kurdish refugees.  Little did I know that I’d be returning to Kuwait and Iraq just over a decade later for round 2, but that’s another story.

JHSU was one of the happiest and highly motivated units that I have served in.  Whilst I like to think that this was down to my leadership, the truth is that we were respected specialists who were mostly left to our own devices.  My Army boss was many miles away and my RAF boss lived on the other side of the airfield.  He came in very handy when it came to resubmitting my application for the Army Pilots’ Course.  As a helicopter instructor himself it was hard to ignore his letter of support.

 

Next time – Becoming an Army Pilot.

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Next time you are parawaiting at Wether Fell, impress your fellow pilots by informing them that a ‘wether’ is a male sheep that has been castrated before reaching maturity.  Parabollox indeed.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

Reserve Repack Day - Sunday 14th January

Well that’s another Dales Club Repack Day done.  A good turnout with 23 flyers attending.  We were in the Gymnasium this time and not the B Hall at Ilkley Grammar School.  Luckily everyone found the right entrance - if some eventually!

Guy Richardsons, Ginger Nomad, led the team of 4 guides to help us and were busy all day.  It proved a good move to have split sessions in the morning and afternoon as this allowed enough room for people to repack in pairs without waiting around for space.  Plus, ensured that the the support team could give attention to more people.  Where possible people were paired up with others that had the same or similar reserve makes and models, and this too aided operations.

It was great to see the teamwork and help people gave each other, as well as, the sociability.  Plus, I’d like to say thank you to everyone for their patience and help when we had to reset the room from the initial confusion with the children's gym class thinking that they’d booked the place.  Things like this can happen with an innocent misunderstanding by the children’s gymnastic coach but this was all sorted out very amicably and we started 20 mins late waiting for some of our own group and not down to this.  Also, thanks everyone for ensuring the gym was clean and tidy throughout and at the end of the day - really helps.

It’s only the 4th time I’ve repacked my reserve and each time is a massive learning curve.  I’m getting better at it and this year had a new reserve to repack.  It’s a Gin G-Lite and proved quite difficult to repack back down from being opened up.  It’s highly non-porous and the material is very thin and slippery.  Great for what it’s designed for, it just took 3 pairs of hands to get into my Supair reserve bag!  Days like this are invaluable in understanding your equipment and learning from others.  One point Ginge made was that when choosing a reserve, people most often ask about price, weight, square or round but hardly ever how easy it is to pack!  One point to think about and I know one flyer on the day appreciated finding out that theirs is one of the easiest.

A few learning points to share with others are the following:

  • Ensure you have repack instructions for your reserve accessible - preferably printed.  What would you do on a trip if you didn’t have this, and essential for the repack day.
  • If you don’t have one, consider buying a spare reserve bag.  Again, what would you do if you used your reserve and didn’t have a bag to put in it to reuse on a trip?
  • Check your reserve connectors.  On the day we saw mailons that were not screwed tightly enough, and others where the connection could move on the maillon, risking added tension which could cause breakage.
  • Reserves can degrade before their recommended expiry term of use.  Therefore, physically inspecting them is essential.  
  • Know your weight for the reserve to be used.  Too light could lead to instability, too heavy could lead to a more severe descent rate or even, the reserve imploding.
  • Many manufacturers recommend repacking every 6 months.  This is not just for inspection but also so that the reserve is opened out and aired, thereby not as ‘sticky’ and opens at its optimum rate.  Each microsecond of time to open in deployment could be vital.  Professional repacks involve the reserve being aired for 24 hours.
  • Guy (Ginger Nomad) and James (Airofix) can do professional repack with certificates which may be required for some organised trips and SIV’s.  These days are a good opportunity to drop off or collect wings and kit for them, even if you’re not repacking your reserve on the day itself.
  • Finally, Guy and James organise donations of condemned reserves to a charity in Africa that use them for mercy flight drops. Apparently, donators are even notified of where and when their reserve is used this one last time.  You may wish to send any unusable reserves to them for this worthwhile cause.

Again, thanks to Guy, James, Christina and Bernard for the support and invaluable guidance.  Plus, to everyone on the day making it a very positive knowledge-building experience.  See you next year.

P.S.  There were 2 left items from the day.  Below photos.  If yours, please contact me direct via Whatsapp or Telegram.

How to manage an incident on the hill

This is an article from Cross Country Magazine.  It’s highly informative and important for everyone to know how they can help and what their role can be to support others in a critical situation.  Click on the picture to be directed to the online article.

Safe flying,

Clive.

sections.title: A New Section in the Pilot Exam
sections.content:
Paul Rowntree reports

Last month I re-sat two sections of the new online pilot exam.  One of them is a new section called Human Factors and Airmanship.  The reason for the addition is the causes of accidents in today’s statistics.  The number of accidents caused by faulty equipment is now very low and increased training and knowledge of the sport has decreased accidents caused by lack of understanding, but the accident rate is the same.  The reason for this is the rise in accidents caused by our decision making.

This link is a 9-page document recently issued by the BHPA highlighting a number of points we probably/hopefully give subconscious thought to before we go flying, but I am sure we are all guilty of pushing the limits of some of them at times.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content: Martin Baxter reports

sections.title: New Old Skywords
sections.content:
Tam reports

Sometime last year a batch of old hard copy black and white editions of Skywords were found (in the library of all places - who’d have thunk it). These date from 2001 - 2004, a time when HG was more prevalent, Trev was Chairman, we had foot and mouth interfering with flying, new fangled things being introduced by Pete Logan - like email groups, and some online information.  There was useful instruction, like how to calculate your XC distance using OS Grid references, and lots of interesting facts - paraglider pilots were once known as jellyfish by HG pilots for instance. You can even find out Fred Winstanley’s favourite pastime. So they are worth a look, and have been added to the online library: https://www.dhpc.org.uk/library/skywords-archive.  Some things haven’t changed much of course. Jake Herbert leading the PG XC League, closely followed by Chris Fountain, for instance. You’ll have to ask Fred yourself if you want to know if his favourite pastime has changed.

sections.title: Skytraxx 5 Review
sections.content:
Ed Cleasby reviews

This my personal opinion of the latest Skytraxx 5, with the popular Skytraxx 2.1 model  used for comparison.

First a bit of backstory as I feel it’s important to understand where dedicated flight instruments now sit, given the dominance of free flight apps, and whether full-fat, dedicated flight instruments are still relevant.

What went before?

For years I flew with the excellent Flymaster … whichever  model you had, they were quality instruments that did everything, but tended to be slow and clunky to use, especially for tasking. Then, about a decade ago, smartphones  began to appear on flightdecks. From around 2012/13 Flyskyhy (iOS) and xctrack (Android) apps came onto the scene and this revolutionised navigation and airspace display along with superb accompanying mapping. Pre-dating them as a sort of intermediate step were xcsoar/LK8000.  In short, despite issues centred on battery life and sunlight visibility, the new apps were way ahead in terms of ease of use, sharing and customisability - but no usable vario. But above all it was the high quality mapping they introduced.  No dedicated instrument, even now, can come close, although a few like the Air 3 piggy-back xctrack,  but at the expense of bulk and weight. Given where things were going, any manufacturer of dedicated flight instruments had an up-hill battle to compete.  Naviter and Flymaster tried; still trying, but with limited success and now seem to be switching to a similar approach to the xctracer … a smaller, compact vario paired with a phone and integrating FLARM, or trying to raise the level of the maps.

A couple of years back, encouraged by the excellent CAA rebate (no-one should have looked this government gift-horse in the mouth) I sold my Flymaster and looked at the options.  Really it boiled down to two … the xctracer maxx and the Skytraxx 2.1. Both had excellent reviews, but for me the Skytraxx offered so much more at the same price; in short, full navigation, airspace and what has always been important to me; it was a fully-fledged backup to my phone (I never feel comfortable with all my eggs in one basket).  My tendency is to always plug in a route (I can’t say I execute many, but I need that focus) and the Skytraxx provides easy tasking, with an igc file having both a g and  c stamp (for declared flights)..  When I bought the 2.1, I pushed for the c record to be added for the UKXL, within two weeks Skytraxx responded with an update. Brilliant! It’s now standard.

My experience of the 2.1 over a number of seasons has been excellent – hard to fault. It’s been totally reliable, easy to use and It's small, light and runs forever - 30hrs/charge. The vario is very responsive, it’s simple to customise, add screens to  - and I like orange. Name another instrument that comes with a bright orange rubber protective cover. It also had (note the use of had) FANET+ hence EC to qualify for the rebate.

Why switch to the Skytraxx 5?

The initial jolt was on discovering that Skytraxx, like other manufacturers, had problems sourcing components to the extent that all models in the Skytraxx  range, from the 2.1 up to and including the 3 and full colour, 4 variants were being discontinued. You can only find second-hand now and they command a premium. A bit of digging also revealed that whilst they were still providing  support, it didn’t cover some parts like screens, keypads or odd chips they couldn’t get hold of.  Hence, the Skytraxx 5 is actually a very different beast in terms of its  internal architecture, with a different keyboard and screen. Basically, a full redesign. However, it keeps all the familiar user features, menu structure and layout of the 2.1, but with some extras including full FLARM, sharing via BT, live tracking and  the Skysafe filter which looks especially useful. Upgrading to full FLARM  means other FLARM users (not just FANET+) appear on the map screen. To avoid clutter, such as with extraneous waypoints, you can be selective in terms of who/what you choose to make visible.

 

What do I think of it?

The first noticeable thing is the screen – another big reason I bought it.  When you put the 2.1 and the 5 side by side (see top photo) the 5 is  larger, sharper and brighter. It’s even more obvious in the flesh.  It’s been slimmed down to a mere 15mm but weight-wise it’s added about 50g to 200gm. Various screen layouts come as defaults, including the Classic 1 from the 2.1, but a new Classic 2 becomes the main start-up screen.You can design as many screen profiles as you need and store them for selection. Within each profile is a nest of screens, all customisable and as many as you wish. One change I’m not a fan of, although you rarely need to use it except to set up your pilot/wing profile, is the navigation around the alphanumeric keyboard. Star Wars fans might love it. It's a gyroscopic tilting wonder to move a small dot … I think I’m pretty good at steady hand games, but I’ve still to develop the knack of moving that darned ball with precision!  The outer case is a sort of hard, non removal rubber - no orange flip off.

Another selling point for me was the vario. The 2.1 is very good, both in terms of responsiveness and having a nice, rising tone. Over the years I played around with the ‘tone editor’ and came up with several ‘Ed’ tones, but in the end I couldn’t better the default.. I did a number of side by side tests and right away it was obvious the 5 is even more responsive and the sound much cleaner. At 50% it’s more than loud enough … a few had complained the 2.1 was rather quiet, although I never found that to be the case running it at 80%.volume I would say the vario is the equal of the xctracer. …. which means the best.

A small addition, not found on the 2.1, is the ‘double tap’ that can be set to go back or forwards through the screens. Whether I’d use it is another matter.

The same four keyboard buttons are retained, although slightly larger and raised slightly – presumably for gloved hands. I actually found the 2.1 keyboard quicker,  the 5 requires a definite push and the scroll speed is slower.  

A few small changes - the hike mode is now subsumed into the main flight logging directory, previously it was separate  and a pain if you were into hike and fly.  I spent XDales time stitching them together!  Now it seems to know if you’re hiking, resting or flying - apparently. A small, but a  useful thing to  note is it now accepts both .wpt and gpx formats. The 2.1 was solely gpx.

These are my initial impressions. I’ve yet to get any flying weather to try it out, but overall I’m really glad I took the opportunity to upgrade. As far as I know there are no dealers in the UK … post Brexit wrecks margins. You’ll need to go direct which can bring its own wallet pain (14 euro to ship in the EU, 50 euro to the UK + duty). Set against  that the rebate is still available until 31st March, but the window is closing.fast.   Like a number of manufacturers there is also a subscription element … Burnair is 39 euro/year although that can be reduced to 20 euro if you opt for a longer period. There is a good online manual (a written one is included, although far less detailed).which explains the benefits, but I don’t see it’s worth it to have live weather, instant flight uploads to X Contest, live tracking etc.  It comes with a sim card inside and the first three months are free so you can try before you decide. However, I will possibly upgrade to full FLARM for a one-off 79 euro.

In my opinion the Skytraxx 5 is well worth considering and you’re very welcome to check out mine. At the end of the day you pay your money. This in my view, is one of the best value, fully dedicated flight instruments available  at  present.Whether you want to pair with your phone, just use it as a  backup, or as a standalone - it will  do everything  that pesky app will do -  minus the mapping, but with five times the battery life and at the lowest weight of  any comparable instrument.

sections.title: Club Communications
sections.content:
Tam reports

The club recently adopted the Telegram Chat Group as a formal club group. Previously it had been left out of formal club comms channels, but as time has progressed it has become clear that it is now a key channel, and will be flagged up in future in new members’ welcome packs etc. The committee continues to believe that longer term info should be put on the relevant area of the forum, so at least to avoid having to answer the same questions multiple times.  Best use would suggest putting info on the forum/website and linking to it from the chat group.

The “Club Communication” article on the website has been updated to reflect this change.

Happy flying all.
